retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Given the increased risk of thromboembolism, patients with clinically active malignancy would benefit from thromboprophylaxis. A meta-analysis looking at a total of 33 trials and 11,972 patients provided more evidence that thromboprophylaxis decreased the incidence of VTE in cancer patients who were undergoing chemotherapy or surgery, while no apparent increase in the incidence of significant bleeding. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: In cancer patients, experiencing a thromboembolic event has serious complications, including increased risk of recurrent thrombosis, increased risk of bleeding during anticoagulation therapy, increased hospitalization rates, and increased mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A recent meta-analysis addressed the question of the optimum duration of anticoagulation in cancer patients who are hospitalized with acute illnesses. This study looked at trials comparing standard-duration versus extended-duration anticoagulation prophylaxis. The risk of VTE was not significantly lower in the extended-duration prophylaxis group of patients but the risk of bleeding was about two-fold higher. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describes the etiology of cancer-associated thrombosis Summarize the diagnostic examination process for cancer-associated thrombosis Reviews current guidelines for the treatment of cancer-associated thrombosis Outlines the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for cancer patients with thromboembolic disease, especially if they are receiving anticoagulation. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer-associated thrombosis increases mortality in cancer patients. In a review of 8 million Medicare patients admitted for VTE, the probability of death within six months of admission for cancer patients was 94% compared to 29% with non-cancer patients. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple factors contribute to the increased risk of arterial and venous thromboembolism in cancer patients: Tumor's tissue and anatomical factors Patient's comorbidities including obesity and advanced age Surgical and medical oncological treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Continuing to assess for benefit vs. risks as well as monitoring for complications is essential. In patients with DVT located in inferior vena cava, iliac, femoral, and popliteal veins in addition to a contraindication to therapeutic anticoagulation, placement of retrievable vena cava filters can prevent pulmonary embolism. Once placed, it is important to periodically assess patients for resolution of contraindication, removal of the vena cava filters, and switching to therapeutic anticoagulation. For patients with catheter-associated thrombosis, treatment consists of removing the catheter or anticoagulation if the catheter remains. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anticoagulation -- Indications -- Treatment of Venous Thromboembolism in Patients with Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Thromboembolism is a frequent complication in cancer patients due to the release of procoagulants from neoplastic cells. Several studies have demonstrated that LMWH is superior to VKAs in the treatment of VTE in cancer patients. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombolytic therapy can be used in patients with life or limb-threatening pulmonary embolism or acute deep vein thrombosis with consideration of the contraindications as intracranial tumors or metastasis, active bleeding, and history of intracranial hemorrhage. NCCN recommends a treatment duration minimum of 3 months or for the duration of active malignancy. For patients with non-catheter-related deep venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, indefinite anticoagulation is the recommendation.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with superficial venous thrombosis, if it is related to peripheral catheter, current NCCN guidelines recommend removing the catheter and consider anticoagulation if thrombus progression occurs. In patients with superficial venous thrombosis non-catheter related especially in lower extremities, recommendations are for at least six weeks of anticoagulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The correlation between malignancy and thrombosis has been reported as early as 1823. Multiple published accounts show an increased risk of VTE in patients with malignancy with prevalence as high as 20%. A large case-control study in the Netherlands examined 3220 patients aged 18 to 70; the study found the overall risk of venous thrombosis to be 7-fold higher in patients with malignancy, the highest risk was in the first few months after diagnosis with the highest risk increase seen in patients with hematological malignancies, lung cancer, gastrointestinal cancer, or with distant metastases. [1] In another case-control study in Minnesota, a 4-fold increase risk of VTE was found in patients with cancer, while patients receiving chemotherapy experienced an even higher risk. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with unprovoked venous thromboembolism as well as patients with arterial thrombosis without identifiable cardiovascular risk factors should always raise suspicion for occult malignancy. Currently, there is insufficient evidence that extensive testing for occult malignancy in patients with unprovoked thrombotic disease would reduce cancer-related mortality. [6] However, age-appropriate cancer screening is necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer-associated thrombosis adds to the complexity of care for cancer patients. The utilization of multidisciplinary care is essential. Primary care physicians, as well as primary oncologist/hematologist, will initiate evaluation and the discussion with the patient regarding benefits vs. risks of anticoagulation therapy if it is needed. They are also responsible for educating patients about side effects and perform close monitoring for risks of bleeding. Pharmacists are necessary to ensure correct dose adjustments—nursing staff, whether inpatient or ambulatory, play a significant role in education and medication administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding outpatient VTE prophylaxis, surgical pelvic or abdominal oncology patients would benefit from continuing VTE prophylaxis up to four weeks post-operation. The use of aspirin or anticoagulation therapy for patients with multiple myeloma on immunomodulatory medications is recommended based on risk stratification with the IMPEDE VTE score. [9] [10] For patients with solid cancers on chemotherapy and high Khorana score, prophylactic anticoagulation with direct oral anticoagulation or low molecular weight heparin showed a decrease in the incidence of pulmonary embolism. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with malignancy often experience increased risk of thrombosis, both venous and arterial. Clinical venous thromboembolism (VTE) remains one of the most common causes of mortality and morbidity among cancer patients. Pathophysiology of this disease stems from the hypercoagulable state related to the increasing production of substances like tissue factor that has high procoagulant activity. The thromboembolic disease can be the first manifestation of occult malignancy or more commonly occur within six months of a cancer diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer patients also face an increased risk of arterial thromboembolism. In a large retrospective study with 279,719 pairs of cancer patients and matched controls, the 6-month incidence of myocardial infarction was 2.0% in cancer patients compared to 0.7% in control patients, while for ischemic stroke, the incidence was 3.0% for cancer patients compared to 1.6% for control patients. The study also found that cancer patients experiencing arterial thromboembolism had a poor prognosis, with a 3-fold increased hazard for death. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 791
retrieved_docs.content: methods, highlighting the use of open and percutaneous arterial or venous access for rapid administration of thrombolytics, chemotherapeutics, pulse‑spray or continuous infusion drug delivery, and transcatheter embolization to occlude abnormal vessels. Together, these monitoring and therapeutic catheter techniques illustrate the essential role of percutaneous arterial and venous access in the assessment and treatment of cardiovascular, surgical, critical‑care, and oncologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: specific cardiovascular diseases, inflammatory pathways, lipid‑modifying and hypoglycemic agents, anticoagulants, and related oncologic and pancreatic conditions intersect, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1637
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is linked to the condition described as احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, which is classified within the broader group of حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ). This cardiac condition is therefore considered a member of the thrombotic and arterial embolism spectrum. Clinical evidence indicates that alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting lipid‑lowering therapy, can be employed to prevent the occurrence of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, highlighting its role in cardiovascular risk reduction. In addition to its cardiovascular relevance, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ shows clinical associations with سرطان المثانة (bladder cancer) and مرض النقرس (gout), suggesting that patients with this cardiac condition may have an increased likelihood of these comorbid diseases or share common pathogenic pathways. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, preventive therapy selection, and coordinated management of patients who present with acute myocardial infarction and related systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 605
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents and function through the inhibition of coagulation factor Xa, a key step in the clotting cascade. This class of drugs exerts its therapeutic effect by directly targeting factor Xa activity, thereby reducing thrombus formation. Rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the use of this mechanism in clinical practice for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: (التخثر الوريدي) is identified as a subtype of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد). This integrated knowledge connects drug classes, contraindications, and therapeutic strategies for shock with the pathophysiology of arterial and venous thromboembolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 212
retrieved_docs.content: combined with excipients, and employed in specialized pharmaceutical procedures, thereby supporting individualized patient care across oncology, immunotherapy, anticoagulation, hormonal therapy, and supportive medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 765
retrieved_docs.content: illustrate that invasive arterial and venous access procedures serve dual purposes—providing diagnostic hemodynamic and electrical data through targeted catheter placement and transesophageal echocardiography, and enabling precise therapeutic delivery of thrombolytic, chemotherapeutic, or embolic agents for vascular and oncologic conditions. The integration of therapeutic vascular catheter techniques with continuous cardiovascular monitoring is presented as essential for optimizing patient assessment, treatment, and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is linked in a broader sense to the concept احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. Both acute myocardial infarction and احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ are classified as members of the SMQ titled حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ), which encompasses thrombotic and arterial embolism cases. In addition, the entity احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ shows clinical associations with سرطان المثانة (bladder cancer) and مرض النقرس (gout). Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand that myocardial injury, as represented by acute myocardial infarction and related cardiac muscle formation processes, occurs within the broader context of vascular thrombotic disorders and may co‑occur with or be influenced by conditions such as bladder cancer and gout, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: of therapeutic or prophylactic substances map to the same catheter‑based interventions, including open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, percutaneous arterial or venous catheterization using pulse‑spray or continuous infusion, and transcatheter embolization. In the venous system, phlebitis, thrombophlebitis, and thromboembolism are classified under venous thrombosis and embolism cases (حالات الخثار والانصمام الوريدية (SMQ)) and are linked to the use of venous catheters (قسطرة وريدية). The interconnections among catheter‑based monitoring, arterial therapeutic procedures, and venous thrombotic disorders are emphasized as essential for comprehensive patient management and for minimizing procedural risks in critical and interventional care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: and their potential toxicity profiles. Collectively, the classification of fibrinolytics, their mechanistic subtypes (plasminogen activators, direct fibrin degraders, platelet‑aggregation inhibitors, and coagulation modulators), their overlap with broader antithrombotic drug classes, the concurrent use of contrast media in fluoroscopic procedures, and the mapped adverse‑effect relationships constitute an integrated pharmacologic and imaging strategy essential for the prevention and treatment of thrombotic vascular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting the activity of factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade. Within this pharmacologic group, rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the direct inhibition of factor Xa to prevent thrombus formation. Understanding that rivaroxaban is a member of the broader Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors category helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy and appreciate the shared mechanism of action among these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: embolism (**حالات الخثار والانصمام الوريدية**) and are connected to **قسطرة وريدية** (intravenous catheter) use, underscoring catheter‑related thrombotic complications. Together, these entities and relationships provide an integrated framework for vascular access, invasive hemodynamic monitoring, interventional therapy, specialty responsibilities, and associated vascular diseases, supporting clear clinical documentation and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 252
retrieved_docs.content: is a subclass of blood‑forming organ drugs and encompasses the more specific group “vitamin K and other hemostatics,” which includes agents that promote clot formation through vitamin K‑dependent pathways. Together, these interrelated classifications illustrate how antithrombotic, hemostatic, and enzymatic agents are organized within the pharmacological landscape of blood and blood‑forming organ therapeutics, providing a structured framework for understanding their mechanisms and clinical applications.
